title: 10 best herbal teas for dandruff
intro_desc: Herbal teas for dandruff are infusions made from plants that can help alleviate symptoms of dandruff, such as itching, flaking, and dryness. These teas can be used as a rinse after shampooing or as a leave-in treatment. Some examples of herbal teas that can help with dandruff include chamomile, calendula, and nettle. Chamomile has anti-inflammatory properties that can soothe an itchy scalp, while calendula and nettle have antifungal and antibacterial properties that can help to balance the scalp's natural oils and reduce flaking. By using herbal teas for dandruff, individuals can improve the health of their scalp and hair, leading to a reduction in dandruff and an improvement in their overall confidence and well-being.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Roman chamomile teas helps with dandruff because its anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties soothe an itchy scalp, reducing redness and irritation. The calming effects of the tea also promote a healthy environment for hair growth, reducing flakiness and scaling associated with dandruff. Additionally, chamomile's natural antioxidants help to neutralize free radicals that can exacerbate scalp issues, leading to a clearer, healthier complexion on the scalp.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties: Roman Chamomile tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as apigenin and luteolin, can help reduce redness and swelling associated with dandruff, while its antiseptic properties combat fungal and bacterial infections that contribute to the condition.

Antioxidant properties: The antioxidants present in Roman Chamomile tea, like flavonoids and phenolic acids, can neutralize free radicals that damage skin cells, helping to reduce irritation and inflammation caused by dandruff.

Soothing and calming effects: The chamazulene and other volatile oils in Roman Chamomile tea have a sedative effect on the skin, which can help calm irritated scalps and reduce itching associated with dandruff.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the Roman Chamomile plant contain anti-inflammatory compounds that help soothe and calm irritated scalps, making them effective in treating dandruff.
Flowers: The flowers of the Roman Chamomile plant have antifungal and antibacterial properties that help to reduce inflammation and prevent fungal infections that can contribute to dandruff.
remedy_recipe: Measure out one teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers or four teaspoons of fresh leaves for every cup water.
Heat filtered water to a temperature of 212°f and pour it over the measured herb in a teapot or infuser.
Allow the mixture to steep for eight to ten minutes, depending on desired strength and flavor.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth if using loose leaves, discard solids.
Serve hot or chilled, with or without honey or lemon to taste, enjoying within one hour of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Littleleaf linden teas helps with dandruff because its antifungal and antibacterial properties soothe an itchy scalp, reducing flaking and inflammation. The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, calm redness and irritation, while its natural astringent properties help tighten the skin, reducing oiliness that can contribute to dandruff. Additionally, linden teas' moisturizing qualities hydrate the scalp, promoting healthy skin cell turnover and alleviating dryness, making it an effective remedy for soothing and calming dandruff-prone scalps.
remedy_properties: Antifungal and antibacterial properties: Littleleaf linden tea's antifungal and antibacterial properties can help to reduce the growth of yeast and bacteria on the scalp, which can contribute to dandruff.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in littleleaf linden tea may help to soothe an itchy and irritated scalp, reducing redness and inflammation associated with dandruff.

Antioxidant properties: Antioxidants present in littleleaf linden tea can help to protect the scalp from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to oxidative stress and exacerbate dandruff symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, roots are used to reduce scalp irritation and promote healthy skin.
Bark: Containing tannins and flavonoids, bark helps soothe and calm the scalp, reducing dandruff symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried leaves from tilia cordata plants to obtain 2-3 tablespoons.
Combine harvested leaves with boiling water in a teapot or infuser to steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid into a cup using a tea strainer or cheesecloth to remove leaves.
Add honey or other sweeteners to taste and stir well to combine for 1 minute.
Pour the prepared tea into a warm cup and serve immediately while still hot.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with dandruff because of its antifungal, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties. The tea's soothing effects can help calm an itchy scalp, reducing redness and flaking associated with dandruff. Additionally, lemon balm's ability to balance the body's natural pH levels can help regulate the skin's natural moisture barrier, preventing dryness and flakiness that contributes to dandruff. By drinking herbal lemon balm tea regularly, individuals may experience relief from the discomfort of dandruff and enjoy a healthier scalp.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Lemon balm tea's antimicrobial properties help to combat fungal infections, such as Malassezia, which can contribute to dandruff and other scalp issues.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in lemon balm tea reduce redness and irritation on the scalp, providing relief from itching and flaking associated with dandruff.

Soothing and calming effects: Lemon balm tea's calming properties help to reduce stress and promote relaxation, which can also alleviate symptoms of dandruff by reducing scalp tension and promoting healthy hair growth.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe itchy scalp and reduce flaking.
Leaves: The leaves contain antioxidants and antifungal compounds that combat fungal infections causing dandruff.
remedy_recipe: Measure one tablespoon of dried melissa officinalis leaves or one teaspoon of fresh leaves.
Heat one cup of water in a pot to a temperature of 195-205°f for 5 minutes.
Add the measured lemon balm leaves to the hot water and let it steep for 7-10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard solids.
Allow the tea to cool for 2-3 minutes before serving hot or chilling in refrigerator.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Horsetail teas helps with dandruff because of its rich content of silica, which is essential for healthy skin and hair. Silica in horsetail tea helps to strengthen hair follicles, reducing shedding and flaking that can lead to dandruff. Additionally, the antioxidant properties of horsetail tea help to soothe and calm the scalp, reducing inflammation and itchiness associated with dandruff.
remedy_properties: Selenium content: Horsetail tea contains selenium, an essential mineral that has antifungal and antibacterial properties, which can help control fungal infections that contribute to dandruff.
Silica content: Horsetail is rich in silica, a natural antioxidant that can help soothe and calm the scalp, reducing inflammation and flaking associated with dandruff.
Antioxidant properties: The antioxidants present in horsetail tea may help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation on the scalp, which can contribute to dandruff and other skin issues.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in silicates, which help to soothe and calm itchy scalps, reducing inflammation associated with dandruff.
Rhyzomes: Contains antioxidants that combat free radicals, promoting healthy skin and scalp conditions.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried equisetum arvense stems and leaves according to your local regulations.
Rinse the harvested herbs with cold water to remove any dirt or debris gently.
Combine one tablespoon of dried horsetail herb or two tablespoons of fresh horsetail herb with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 10-15 minutes to allow the flavors and nutrients to infuse fully slowly.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard the solids before serving immediately.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Stinging nettle teas helps with dandruff because its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties soothe an itchy scalp, reducing inflammation and redness associated with flaky skin. The tea's ability to calm irritation also reduces the amount of oil produced by the scalp, helping to normalize sebum production and prevent excessive dryness that can lead to flakes. As a result, stinging nettle teas help to promote healthy hair growth while effectively tackling dandruff symptoms.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Stinging nettle tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as naringenin and scopoletin, can reduce redness and swelling associated with dandruff, providing relief from itching and irritation.

Antimicrobial: The tea's antimicrobial properties, including its ability to inhibit fungal and bacterial growth, can help combat the underlying causes of dandruff, such as Malassezia yeast overgrowth and bacteria on the scalp.

Soothing and moisturizing: Stinging nettle tea's rich content of vitamins A, C, and E, as well as its ability to soothe skin irritations, can help calm and hydrate the scalp, reducing flaking and dandruff symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, roots are effective in soothing itchy scalps and reducing inflammation associated with dandruff.
Flowers: Abundant in flavonoids and phenolic acids, flowers provide a calming and antiseptic properties that help to reduce redness and irritation on the scalp.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh or dried stinging nettle leaves measuring one teaspoon per eight ounces of boiling water.
Combine gathered nettle leaves with boiled and cooled water in a heat-resistant container for steeping.
Allow the mixture to steep for seven to ten minutes then strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve.
Add honey or lemon juice to taste after the tea has cooled slightly following traditional folk remedy practices.
Store any remaining tea in an airtight container and consume within 24 hours of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion teas helps with dandruff because it has natural anti-inflammatory properties that soothe an itchy scalp, reducing redness and irritation. The antioxidants present in dandelion tea also help to calm and balance the skin's natural pH levels, which can become imbalanced due to excessive oil production or dryness. Additionally, dandelion tea's antifungal and antibacterial properties work to combat the underlying causes of dandruff, such as fungal infections and bacterial buildup on the scalp.
remedy_properties: Antifungal and antibacterial properties: Dandelion tea's antifungal and antibacterial properties can help combat scalp infections and reduce flaking caused by fungal or bacterial overgrowth, thereby alleviating symptoms of dandruff.

Anti-inflammatory effects: The anti-inflammatory compounds present in dandelion tea can soothe itchy scalps, reduce redness and irritation, and calm inflammation associated with dandruff, providing relief from discomfort and itchiness.
Detoxifying properties: Dandelion tea's diuretic and choleretic effects can help remove toxins and excess oil from the scalp, which can contribute to flaking and itching in people with dandruff.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, dandelion roots help soothe itchy scalps and reduce inflammation associated with dandruff.
Seeds: Dandelion seeds are a natural antifungal agent, making them effective in combating fungal infections that contribute to dandruff.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried dandelion flowers and leaves according to local regulations and guidelines.
Combine one tablespoon of dried dandelion flowers with one cup of boiling water in a teapot.
Steep the mixture for 10-15 minutes to release the bioactive compounds and volatile oils.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids and sediment.
Enjoy the tea hot or cold, sweetened with honey if desired, after 30-60 minutes of preparation time.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Rosemary teas helps with dandruff because of its antibacterial and antifungal properties that help to soothe and calm the scalp. The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds reduce redness and itching, while its antioxidant properties promote healthy hair growth and prevent damage caused by free radicals. Additionally, rosemary tea's ability to increase blood flow to the scalp helps to unclog pores and remove dead skin cells, leaving the scalp clean and flake-free.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial Properties: Rosemary's antimicrobial compounds, such as camphor and bornyl acetate, help combat fungal and bacterial infections on the scalp that can contribute to dandruff.
Anti-Inflammatory Effects: Rosemary's anti-inflammatory properties, including rosmarinic acid, reduce redness and irritation associated with dandruff, promoting a healthy scalp environment.
Antioxidant Activity: Rosemary tea's antioxidant compounds, such as caffeic acid, help protect the scalp from free radical damage and oxidative stress, which can exacerbate dandruff symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, making them effective in soothing scalp irritation and reducing flaking.
Leaves: High in essential oils with antifungal and antibacterial properties, helping to combat fungal infections that can cause dandruff.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried rosemary leaves from the rosmarinus officinalis plant for a cup of tea.
Combine one teaspoon of dried rosemary leaves or two teaspoons of fresh leaves with boiling water in a teapot.
Steep the rosemary mixture in hot water for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors and aromas to infuse.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the herb residue.
Serve the rosemary tea hot, sweetened with honey if desired, and enjoy within 20 minutes of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sage teas helps with dandruff because of its natural anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties. The antioxidants present in sage tea help to soothe an itchy scalp, reducing inflammation and redness associated with dandruff. Additionally, the tea's antimicrobial compounds work to combat fungal infections that can contribute to dandruff, promoting a healthy scalp environment and alleviating symptoms such as flaking and itching. Regular consumption of sage tea may help to calm and balance the scalp, leading to a reduction in dandruff flakes and an overall improvement in scalp health.
remedy_properties: Antifungal Properties: Sage tea's antifungal properties help combat fungal infections that can contribute to dandruff, reducing flaking and itching by inhibiting the growth of yeast and other microorganisms.

Anti-Inflammatory Properties: Sage tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as camphor and bornyl acetate, reduce redness, swelling, and irritation caused by dandruff, promoting a healthy scalp and soothing itching.

Antioxidant Activity: Sage tea's high antioxidant content helps protect the scalp from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to skin conditions like dandruff and promote overall scalp health.
remedy_parts: Roots: Sage roots have been traditionally used to treat scalp conditions, including dandruff, due to their antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties.
Leaves: Sage leaves contain antioxidants and volatile oils that help soothe and calm irritated scalps, reducing flaking and itchiness associated with dandruff.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh or dried sage leaves from reputable sources in quantities of one ounce per quart of water.
Measure and combine one teaspoon of dried sage or two teaspoons of fresh sage with boiling water.
Steep the sage mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow flavors and essential oils to infuse into the water.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids and capture flavorful liquid.
Serve the herbal sage tea hot, sweetened with honey if desired, and enjoy its calming and digestive benefits.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thyme teas helps with dandruff because of its antifungal and antibacterial properties, which effectively combat the underlying fungal infections that cause this condition. The antioxidants present in thyme also soothe the scalp, reducing inflammation and itchiness associated with dandruff. Additionally, thyme's natural anti-inflammatory compounds help to reduce redness and scaling, promoting a healthy and balanced scalp environment that is less prone to dandruff.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Thyme tea's antimicrobial properties help to kill fungal and bacterial infections that can cause dandruff, reducing flaking and itching.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in thyme tea reduce redness and swelling associated with dandruff, soothing the scalp and promoting a healthy environment for hair growth.
Antioxidant properties: Thyme tea's antioxidants help to neutralize free radicals that can damage the scalp and contribute to dandruff, while also promoting overall scalp health and well-being.
remedy_parts: Roots: Thyme roots have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe and calm irritated scalp, making them an effective ingredient in teas for dandruff treatment.
Bark: The bark of the thyme plant contains salicylic acid, a compound similar to aspirin, which helps to reduce inflammation and itchiness associated with dandruff.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality fresh or dried thymus vulgaris leaves from local sources or stores.
Measure one teaspoon of dried thyme leaves or two tablespoons of fresh thyme leaves for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Combine measured thyme leaves with boiling water in a teapot or heat-resistant cup and steep for 5 to 10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids and preserve flavor.
Serve hot or allow the tea to cool to room temperature and refrigerate for up to 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marjoram teas helps with dandruff because it has natural antifungal and antibacterial properties that help to soothe and calm the scalp, reducing inflammation and irritation that can lead to flaking. The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds also help to reduce redness and itching associated with dandruff, allowing the skin to heal and recover more effectively.
remedy_properties: Antifungal properties: Marjoram tea's antifungal compounds, such as thymol and borneol, help combat fungal infections on the scalp that can contribute to dandruff.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Marjoram tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as rosmarinic acid and carnosic acid, reduce redness and swelling associated with dandruff, promoting a healthy scalp environment.
Antioxidant properties: Marjoram tea's antioxidant compounds help protect the scalp from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which can exacerbate dandruff symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that can help soothe itchy scalp and reduce inflammation associated with dandruff.
Leaves: Containing flavonoids and terpenes, which have antifungal and antibacterial properties, helping to combat fungal infections and balance the scalp's natural flora.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried marjoram leaves in quantities of 2 tablespoons for every 8 ounces of water needed.
Rinse the harvested leaves gently with cold running water to remove any dirt or debris.
Heat filtered water to boiling point and let it steep for 5-7 minutes with the marjoram leaves.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup or teapot and discard the solids.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving hot, sweetening if desired, and enjoying within 30 minutes of preparation.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat dandruff with herbal teas is by using chamomile or green tea. Both teas have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties that can soothe an itchy scalp and help reduce dandruff. To use, steep the tea in hot water, let it cool, and then rinse your hair with it after shampooing. For chamomile tea, you can also use the cooled tea as a final hair rinse and leave it on for 10-15 minutes before rinsing it out. Repeat this process once or twice a week for best results.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to dandruff that are treated with herbal teas include seborrheic dermatitis and psoriasis. These conditions can cause itchiness, redness, and flaking of the scalp, similar to dandruff. Herbal teas, such as chamomile and nettle, have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that can help alleviate these symptoms. Chamomile tea can soothe the scalp and reduce inflammation, while nettle tea can help regulate sebum production and promote hair growth. To use, steep the tea in hot water, let it cool, and then use it as a rinse after shampooing. It's important to note that while herbal teas can help alleviate symptoms, they should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment. Always consult with a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment.
